coredata.dc:title: A sensitive colorimetric sensor for glutathione on the basis of the oxidase-like activity of polyoxometalate-based helical compound and its nanocomposite with SWNT-COOH
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Mingqi
Wang, Yuguang
Cai, Yunheng
Zhang, Xikun
Wang, Yunliang
Sha, Jingquan
coredata.dc:description: Here, a new Keggin-type POMs based supramolecular compound with the 4-fold meso-helixes, [PW12O40]4MI·3H2O (PWMI) (MI = 2-methylimizole), was successfully synthesized and characterized by routine method. As one new kind of nanozymes, compound PWMI was found to possess oxidase-like activity for the first time, namely, it can effectively catalyze the oxidation of the substrate 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB). With functionalized single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWNTs-COOH) introduced into PWMI, the oxidase-like activity of nanocomposite PWMI/SWNTs-COOH was further enhanced. More importantly, the PWMI/SWNTs-COOH-based colorimetric sensor exhibits the fastest response time (30 s) among all reported nanozymes for glutathione (GSH) detection to date, to the best our knowledges, and the limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.017 μM, and the linear range as wide as 5 to 150 μM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyoxometalates
Supramolecular
SWNT-COOH
Helix
Glutathione

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Ball/sticks/ polyhedral representation of 4-fold meso-helices: type I is constructed by PW12, MI, and H2O; type II is constructed by PW12, MI, and H2O.
(a) PXRD patterns of the as-prepared samples. TEM images of SWNT-COOH (b), PWMI/SWNT-COOH (c and d).

(a) UV − vis absorption spectra of in TMB oxidation catalyzed by different reaction systems and controls in an acetate buffer solution (insert: corresponding optical photographs) (b) Dependence of the oxidase-mimicking activity on PWMI/SWNT-COOH dosage, reaction time, temperature, and pH (dosage: 0–8 mg; time: 1–12 min; temperature: 25–50 ℃; pH: 3.0–7.0; TMB dosage: 30–180 µL).

UV − vis absorption spectra and optical photographs of the peroxidase-like catalytic reaction systems for PWMI (a) and PWMI/SWNT-COOH (c) with varied GSH concentrations. The response curve for PWMI (b) and PWMI/SWNT-COOH (d) to determine GSH with the inset displaying the linear calibration curve from 0 to 150 μM.
Schematic illustration of detection of GSH based on the oxidase-like activity of polyoxometalate-based helical compound and its SWNT-COOH nanocomposite PWMI/SWNT-COOH.
Performance’s comparison of PWMI, PWMI/SWNT-COOH and other reported oxidase-mimics.

head.ce:title: A sensitive colorimetric sensor for glutathione on the basis of the oxidase-like activity of polyoxometalate-based helical compound and its nanocomposite with SWNT-COOH

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Jiamusi University, HeilongJiang, Jiamusi 154007, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Jiamusi University, HeilongJiang, Jiamusi 154007, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Functional Materials in Universities of Shandong, Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jining University, Qufu, Shandong 273155, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Functional Materials in Universities of Shandong
Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Functional Materials in Universities of Shandong, Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jining University, Qufu, Shandong 273155, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Compound PWMI with 4-fold meso-helixes were synthesized and found to possess oxidase-like activity for the first time, and its SWNT-COOH nanocomposites exhibit the fastest response time towards the detection of GSH to date.

Here, a new Keggin-type POMs based supramolecular compound with the 4-fold meso-helixes, [PW12O40]4MI·3H2O (PWMI) (MI = 2-methylimizole), was successfully synthesized and characterized by routine method. As one new kind of nanozymes, compound PWMI was found to possess oxidase-like activity for the first time, namely, it can effectively catalyze the oxidation of the substrate 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB). With functionalized single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWNTs-COOH) introduced into PWMI, the oxidase-like activity of nanocomposite PWMI/SWNTs-COOH was further enhanced. More importantly, the PWMI/SWNTs-COOH-based colorimetric sensor exhibits the fastest response time (30 s) among all reported nanozymes for glutathione (GSH) detection to date, to the best our knowledges, and the limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.017 μM, and the linear range as wide as 5 to 150 μM.

ce:sections.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH), a thiol-containing tripeptide (glutamic acid, cysteine, and glycine), widely exists in mammalian and eukaryotic cells [1,2], and its disordered cellular levels may lead to many diseases, such as liver damage, cancer, and so on [3–4]. Therefore, diverse detection techniques have been reported in the recent years [5–7]. Among, the colorimetric biosensing technique has been attracting positive attention, because of its many virtues, such as cheapness, satisfactory sensitivity and selectivity, and so on [8–10], since the Fe3O4 nanoparticles were found to possess peroxidase-mimic activities for the first time [11]. As we all know, nanomaterials as artificial enzymes (nanozymes) exhibited different mimic activities in colorimetric biosensing, including peroxidase- [12], oxidase- [13], catalase- [14], and so on. Compared with others [12–14,15], oxidase-like nanozymes possess more attractive superiority for biosensors, because of no additional auxiliary reagents in the reaction system [16]. However, two issues can be found through extensive literatures, namely, one is that the most reported GSH colorimetric detection rely on peroxidase-like catalytic reaction system [17,18]. Another is that oxidase-like nanozymes generally require longer time to achieve catalytic properties due to the less and slower formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [19]. As a result, developing the oxidase-like nanozymes with faster response time for GSH detection was highly desired.

Polyoxometalates (POMs) as one of the most important inorganic metal-oxo clusters possess not only rich structural versatility but also reversible multi-electron redox activities [20], which have widely applied in colorimetric sensing as nanozymes, such as H3PW12O40 [21], H4SiW12O40 [22], (NH4)3[H6Fe(III)Mo6O24] [23] and Na10H2W12O42 [24]. Moreover, supramolecular chemistry has been used to understand the intermolecular interactions and to design new crystalline compounds with unprecedented structures and desired properties [25], particularly supramolecular compounds with helical structures [26–28]. Since the first vanadium phosphate with helix was reported by Zubieta in 1993[29], many such crystalline compounds were reported and applied in photo-catalysis, electro-catalysis, medicine, and so on [30–32]. Unfortunately, little attention has been paid to POMs-based supramolecular compounds with helix as nanozymes [33].

In this work, a Keggin-type POMs based supramolecular compound with the 4-fold meso-helixes, [PW12O40]4MI·3H2O (PWMI) (MI = 2-methylimizole), was synthesized and characterized by routine method. The compound PWMI as nanozyme was found to possess oxidase-like activity for the first time, which exhibits the lower limit of detection (LOD, 0.038 μM) and wider linear range (5–150 μM) for GSH detection. Considering the poor electronic conductivity of PWMI, PWMI was also integrated with functionalized single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWNTs-COOH) into nanocomposite PWMI/SWNTs-COOH, which shows improved catalytic performance (0.017 μM of LOD) and the shortest response time (30 s) among all reported nanozymes as GSH colorimetric biosensors to date (Scheme 1 ).

The title compound was prepared by the reaction from a mixture of H3PW12O40, 2-methylimizole (MI), Ni(NO3)2, NH4VO3 and PEG-2000 at 170 °C for 4 days.[34] Note that no crystals were obtained in the absence of PEG-2000 or NH4VO3. Single-crystal X-ray structural analysis [35] reveals that PWMI crystallizes in the triclinic space group P-1 and consists of one [PW12O40]3− polyoxoanion (abbreviated to PW12), four MI molecules, and three H2O molecules (Fig.S1a). In detail, there are four crystallography independent MI molecules (MI-1, MI-2, MI-3, MI-4), and their coordination modes are illustrated in Fig. S1b, in which each MI-1, MI-2, MI-3 with four O atoms exhibits tetradentate coordination mode, and MI-4 with seven O atoms shows 7-coordination modes. Consequently, each PW12 links with four N ions and four C ions from MI, three O ions from H2O molecules and three O from the other PW12 by supramolecular interactions, exhibiting the richer coordination mode (Fig.S1c). Note that supramolecular interactions play vital roles to stabilize the structure of PWMI, which build up the three-dimensional framework of PWMI (Fig.S2). It is interesting that two types of helix can be observed in the supramolecular framework of PWMI (Fig. 1 ), namely, PW12, MI and H2O construct the first type of meso-helical chains with 12.1520 Å pitch of screw along a axis (type I), and the left- and right-helix possesses the same route {- PW12…O3W…MI-2…PW12…MI-4…PW12…MI-3…PW12…MI-1-}. Taking no account of MI, PW12 with H2O fabricate type II the left and right-handed meso-helices along the same route of {- PW12 (O24) …(O32) PW12(O30) …O3W…(O38) PW12-} with the 12.1520 Å thread pitch.

The structural correctness and purity of PWMI was certified by powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) measurement. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the experimental PXRD pattern of PWMI is in good agreement with the simulated one, indicating that the collected samples are pure. Moreover, PWMI NC and PWMI/SWNT-COOH exhibit similar PXRD patterns to that of PWMI, confirming that the basic structure of PWMI is well retained during the reaction. Unfortunately, peaks belonging to SWCNT-COOH are not found, probably due to its low levels. The FT-IR spectra of PWMI and PWMI/SWNT-COOH feature several characteristic bands at 1050, 957, 855 and 784 cm−1, attributed to the P-O, W-Ot, W-Ob-W and W-Oc-W stretching vibrations of PW12 polyanions, respectively (Fig. S3). Moreover, peaks belonging to SWCNT-COOH show slight movements in PWMI/SWNT-COOH nanocomposite, indicating the successful combination of PWMI with SWCNT-COOH. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the functionalized SWCNT-COOH show a smooth surface (Fig. 2b), but TEM and HR-TEM of PWMI/SWNT-COOH nanocomposite show externally grafted substances, indicating SWNT-COOH bundles are decorated by PWMI (Fig. 2c and d). Finally, considering the PWMI as catalytical materials in the work, their physical and chemical stability were explored by PXRD (Fig. S4), and results indicate that PWMI is stable in the pH range of 3–7 for 24 h at room temperature.

To investigate their oxidase-like activity, as-prepared PWMI, SWNT-COOH and PWMI/SWNT-COOH were directly incubated with 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB). Note that the absorbance peaks of PWMI and PWMI/SWNT-COOH are at ca. 308 nm (Fig. S5), when adding them into TMB solution, colorless TMB solution changes to blue, and the absorbance peak at 659 nm gradually increase, indicating the formation of oxTMB (Fig. 3 a) [11]. This fact demonstrates that PWMI and PWMI/SWNT-COOH possess the oxidase-like activity, and the oxidase-like performance of PWMI/SWNT-COOH is greatly improved after integrating with SWNT-COOH. As comparison, the SWNT-COOH-TMB system is almost colorless under the same conditions, indicating that SWNT-COOH cannot catalyze TMB oxidation directly. As we know, the possible mechanism of the oxidase-like activity of nanozymes may originate from the catalytic ability for activation of O2 to generate ROS in the TMB oxidation reaction [19,23]. To investigate the ROS in the PWMI TMB system, the p-Benzoquinone (BQ) and Iso-propyl alcohol (IPA) were applied as scavengers for the •O2 – and •OH, respectively. Consequently, the catalytic activity of the PWNI-TMB-BQ system is reduced by 21.7%, while that of PWNI-TMB-IPA is only reduced by 7%, the fact indicates that •O2 – is the mainly oxidant in the PWMI-TMB system (Fig. S6). As comparison, the fluorescence intensity decreased by 41% with BQ and 9% with IPA in the PWMI/SWNT-COOH-TMB system, indicating the formation of more •O2 –. This is because the addition of SWNT-COOH facilitate the absorption of O2 due to more defective sites on its surface induced by the functionalized SWNTs (SWNT-COOH), at the same time, its high electrical conductivity accelerates the conversion of O2 to •O2 –, thus enhancing the oxidase-like activity of PWMI.

To achieve optimum performances of the PWMI/SWNT-COOH for GSH detection, we investigated the dependence of absorbance changes of oxTMB on several important parameters, including pH, time, temperature, catalyst dosage and the concentration of TMB. As shown in Fig. 3b and Fig.S7, by varying the pH from 3.0 to 7.0, the reaction time from 0 to 14 min, the temperature from 25 to 50 ℃, the catalyst dosage from 0 to 8 mg, and the TMB dosage from 30 µL to 180 µL (2 mM), we found that the optimum catalytic activity could be achieved at pH = 4.0, temperature 35℃. It is worthwhile mentioning that H3PW12O40 shows the best oxidase-like activity at pH 4.0 [24], and the oxidase activity of PWMI/SWNT-COOH shows little difference at pH 3.5 and pH 4. So pH value 4.0 was selected as the optimal condition. Additionally, the absorbance intensities were firstly rapid increased at the time 0–2 min, the catalyst dosage 0–2 mg and the TMB dosage 0–90 µL, then slightly increased with these parameters further increasing, respectively. Therefore, time 2 min, catalyst 2 mg and TMB 90 μL were selected as the optimal condition in the following experiments. Note that PWMI/SWNT-COOH exhibits the fastest response time for GSH detection under the optimal condition, only 30 s by the naked eye, among all reported nanozymes including the PWMI (2 min), to the best our knowledge (Table 1 ). The excellent oxidase-like activity can be summed up as follows: (1) the multi-electron redox nature of PW12 polyanions in PWMI and supramolecular helical structure with high structural stability and good biocompatibility. (2) more defective sites on its surface induced by the functionalized SWNTs (SWNT-COOH). As a result, O2 is inclined to be preferentially adsorbed by the defect sites. (3) the rational pathway for O2 moving toward active centers due to the introduction and the loose packing of SWNT-COOH into PWMI, then improve the electron transfer rate.

Based on the catalytic activities of PWMI and PWMI/SWNT-COOH and the reducing ability of GSH, a colorimetric method to detect GSH can be constructed. As shown in Fig. 4 , after incubation with PWMI NC or PWMI/SWNT-COOH in pH 4.0 buffer at 35 °C for 2 min, the absorbance of the system at 659 nm gradually decreased with the increasing GSH concentration. In PWMI-TMB system, there is good linear relationship between ΔA (ΔA = Ablank − AGSH) in the concentration of GSH from 5 to 150 μM, and the similar situation can be found in the PWMI/SWNT-COOH-TMB system. By calculation, the limit of GSH detection (LOD) is 0.038 μM for PWMI and 0.017 μM for PWMI/SWNT-COOH. (LOD = KS0/S, where S0 is the SD of the blank measurements (n = 10, K = 3)). The LOD of PWMI/SWNT-COOH is lower than that of Co, N-HPC (0.036 μM) and other reported oxidase mimics (Table 1), implying that the PWMI/SWNT-COOH is a promising nanozyme with excellent sensing capability for GSH.

The selectivity of GSH detection was further investigated by using potential interferences, including L-cysteine (L-Cys), homocysteine (Hcy), glucose (Glu), alanine (Ala), glycine (Gly), lysine (Lys), Urio acid (UA), ascorbic acid (AA), tyrosine (Tyr), D-serine (Ser), Na+, K+ and Ca2+. As shown in Fig. S8, the concentration of interfering substances exceeded that of GSH by 20-fold, but most induced a negligible response in the PWMI/SWNT-COOH-TMB system. Although certain amount of GSH, L-Cys and Hcy could induce the decrease of absorbance intensity due to the thiol groups in their molecular structures, their concentrations in human serum are obviously lower than that of GSH [42]. These results clearly demonstrate that this PWMI/SWNT-COOH-TMB still has a great possibility to be used for reliable GSH detection in practical samples such as human serum. In order to evaluate the feasibility, we test content of GSH in human serum under the optimum condition, and results are listed in Table S2, in which the data of recovery is between 92.64 % and 107.01 %, and detection RSD is in the range of 0.78% to 2.88%. The fact also demonstrates the potential applications of the PWMI/SWNT-COOH-TMB sensor in clinical determination of GSH.

In summary, a new Keggin-type POMs based supramolecular compound with the 4-fold meso-helixes (PWMI) and its SWNT-COOH nanocomposites (PWMI/SWNT-COOH) were successfully prepared. Owing to the excellent multi-electron redox of PW12, PWMI exhibits a certain level of oxidase-like activities. And PWMI/SWNT-COOH nanocomposite as oxidase-like nanozyme exhibits excellent catalytical activity, which is 2 times higher than that of PWMI, maybe due to the more defective sites, a rational pathway for O2 moving toward active centers provided by SWNT-COOH, and their synergy effect. Based on the selective experiment and analysis in human serum samples, it is proved that this method can be used for reliable GSH detection in practical samples. The strategy behind PWMI and PWMI based nanocomposites as colorimetric sensor will promote the further development of POMs based supramolecular compound with helix in biosensors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial supports from Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (LH2020B003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Crystallographic data for the structure reported in this paper have been deposited with Cambridge Crystallographic Data Center as supplementary publication no. CCDC: 2120711. Supplementary data associated with this article: the detailed experimental synthesis, measurements of materials, crystallographic data, IR and PXRD, trapping experiments of active species, selectivity and analyses of glutathione detection are given in Supporting Information. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2022.109212.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: H3PW12O40 (300 mg, 0.16 mmol), 2-methylimizole (MI, 60 mg, 0.73 mmol), Ni(NO3)2 (150 mg, 0.52 mmol), NH4VO3 (36 mg, 0.31 mmol) and PEG-2000 (35 mg) were with stirred for 1 h at room temperature, and pH value was adjusted to ca. 2.5 by 1 M HCl. The resulting solution was transferred and sealed in a 20 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel reactor and heated at 170 ℃ for 4 days. After the autoclave was cooled to room temperature at 10 ℃.h-1, the dark block crystals were obtained, and then washed with distilled water and air-dried (yield: 44% based on W). Elemental analysis: C16H18Ni4N8O43PW40 (8629.69). Anal. Calcd: H, 0.207; C, 2.225; N, 0.649 (%). Found: H, 0.186; C, 2.109; N, 0.598 (%).

Crystal data for PWMI: C16H18Ni4N8O43PW40, Mr = 2174.49, triclinic, space group P-1, a = 12.152(7), b = 12.214(7), c = 20.508(13) Å, α = 84.266(10), β = 85.501(8), γ = 67.025(13)°, V = 2786(3) Å3, Z = 2, T = 296 K, F(000) = 2030. The final R 1 and wR 2 were 0.1081 and 0.2740 respectively [I>2sigma(I)]. Crystallographic data were collected on the Bruker SMART-CCD diffractometer with Mo-Kα radiation (λ = 0.71073 Å) at 296 K. Their structures were solved by the direct method and refined full-matrix last squares on F2 through the SHELXTL and WINGX software package. The CCDC reference number is 2120711.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Bano, V. Kumar, V.K. Singh, S. Chandra, D.K. Singh, P.K. Yadav, M. Talat, S.H. Hasan, ACS Sustainable Chem. Eng., 7 (2018) 1923-1932.
J. Liu, L.J. Meng, Z.F. Fei, P.J.Dyson, X.A. Jing, X. Liu, Biosens Bioelectron. 90 (2017) 69-74.
J.Q. Xi, C.H. Zhu, Y.Q. Wang, Q.N. Zhang, L. Fan, RSC Adv. 9 (2019) 16509-16514.
P. Jin, X.Y. Niu, F. Zhang, K. Dong, H.X. Dai, H.G. Zhang, W.F. Wang, H.L. Chen, X.G. Chen, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 12 (2020) 20414-20422.
S.Q. Li, L.T. Wang, X.D. Zhang, H.X. Chai, Y.M. Huang,Sens. Actuators, B264 (2018) 312-319.
M.J. Huang, H. Wang, D.P. He, P. Jiang, Y. Zhang, Chem. Commun. 2019 (55) 3634-3637.
W. Gao, Z. Liu, L. Qi, J. Lai, S.A. Kitte, G. Xu, Anal. Chem. 88 (2016) 7654–7659.
